meta.title: Guqin Finger-Technique (Hand-Gesture) Reference
meta.description: A categorized, cross-linked reference of guqin (古琴) finger techniques in jianzipu (减字谱) tablature. Each card is self-contained: the full character, pinyin, the handdrawn reduced glyph, a plain-language instruction, demonstration photo/video where available, the related techniques to compare, and the traditional poetic gesture-image where one exists.
meta.note: Explanations synthesized from Pei-You Chang (peiyouqin.com), John Thompson (silkqin.com) and Chinese-language qin sources. Demonstration photos and handdrawn notation glyphs reproduced from peiyouqin.com; gesture woodblocks from silkqin.com. See assets/photos/README.txt and resources.json for attribution.

categories.title: Finger & Hand Designations
categories.subtitle: 左手指法 — which finger presses
categories.description: Abbreviated finger-name components that appear in the upper-left of a jianzipu glyph to tell the left hand which finger presses. Building blocks rather than strokes.

categories.title: Right-Hand Basic Strokes
categories.subtitle: 八法 — the eight basic plucks
categories.description: The core plucking vocabulary. Each of the four fingers and the thumb plucks either inward (toward the player) or outward (away).

categories.title: Right-Hand Compound Strokes
categories.subtitle: Multi-string & rapid figures
categories.description: Pinches, wheels, locks, sweeps and rolls built from the eight basic strokes across one or more strings.

categories.title: Left-Hand Six Basics
categories.subtitle: The fundamentals — in nearly every piece
categories.description: Pei-You Chang organizes the left hand around six fundamentals: two vibratos and four slides. Highest-priority entries.

categories.title: Vibrato Variations
categories.subtitle: Sub-types of 吟 yín and 猱 náo
categories.description: Variations of the two basic vibratos: longer, finer, calmer, faster, immediate, or accented.

categories.title: Slides, Strikes & Ornaments
categories.subtitle: Advanced left-hand motion
categories.description: Slow slides, springy strikes-against, and back-and-forth ornaments performed by the left hand.

categories.title: Left-Hand Plucking & Percussive
categories.subtitle: The left hand makes the sound
categories.description: Pull-offs, hammer-ons, pushes and advance/return slides where the left hand itself produces the note with no right-hand pluck.

categories.title: Tone-Type & Position Designations
categories.subtitle: Context glyphs
categories.description: Glyphs that classify the sound (open / harmonic / stopped) or specify the hui position and string number. Included so a jianzipu reader is complete.

techniques.instruction_text: Thumb. Presses with the side of the thumb where nail meets skin, or the side of the knuckle.
Index finger. Used mostly for harmonics (light touch); sometimes with the thumb.
Middle finger. Used more often on the 1st string.
Ring finger. Presses with the left side where nail meets skin — not the fingertip. Thumb should not rise.
"Kneeling" ring finger — press with the back of the nail / back of the first knuckle, usually above the 5th hui.
Using your index finger to pluck the string.

Using your index finger to strum the string. When playing, slightly bend the index finger and put your index finger tip against your thumb nail tip to strum the string. When using Muǒ and Tiaǒ, be sure your elbow is hanging flat and do not against your wrist on the instrument.

Using your middle finger to pluck the string. Slightly bend the middle finger, and put more strength on the finger tip. After pluck the string, stay your middle finger on the next string and not move away yet.
techniques.poetic_image: Lone duck looks back at the flock (孤鶩顧群)

techniques.instruction_text: Using your middle finger to strum the string. Gou and Ti are a little bit stronger than Muǒ and Tiaǒ. Also keep your elbow hanging flat.
Using your ring finger to pluck the string.

Using your ring finger to strum the string. The ring finger has less power, therefore it has to practice more to get strength. Dă and Zhai are lighter than Muǒ, Tiaǒ, Gou and Ti. Main Menu / 1 / 2 / 3 / Copyright ©2001-2013 Judy (Pei-You) Chang
Thumb plucks inward.
techniques.notes: Historic in/out confusion between 擘/劈 and 托 — see footnote 11 on https://www.silkqin.com/02qnpu/05tydq/ty3.htm. Inward is the modern reading.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your thumb to pluck the string. Bò and Tuo are used more often on the 6th and the 7th strings.
techniques.notes: Outward is the modern reading; see historical in/out note on B7.

techniques.instruction_text: Using the index finger to strum (Tiaǒ) and the middle finger to pluck (Gou) two strings together. The strength must be the same.
Older term for a simultaneous two-string sound, now replaced by 撮 cuō.

On the same string, use the ring finger, middle finger and index finger to do Zhai, Ti, Tiaǒ 3 continuing sound. When playing this technique, bend the 3 fingers slightly, then strum the string gradually. Try to make it strong and clear.
Same technique as Luń but one sound less than Luń. On the same string, use either Zhai Ti or Ti Tiaǒ. Notation: (or 口一)
"Lock" — a rapid repeated cluster of strokes on one string.
On the same string, do the Muǒ Tiaǒ, Muǒ Gou 4 sound first then follow with the Tì Muǒ Tiaǒ 3 continuing sound.

Quán fú (full fú) is a four-note connected figure: the index finger does mǒ across two adjacent strings in one motion, then immediately the middle finger does gōu across the same two strings, while the ring finger lightly stops the residual sound of the first string. The result is four soft, connected sounds that flow as one gesture.

Bàn fú (half fú) is the abbreviated form of quán fú: the index finger does mǒ on the two strings and the middle finger does gōu on the lower of the two strings, producing fewer (typically two) connected soft sounds rather than the full four. It is the lighter, shorter partner of quán fú.
A linked double-pluck (mo+gou feel) on adjacent strings; compare quanfu/banfu.
Successive/linked juan.

Bō and là are an inseparable pair (撥剌). Bō: the index, middle and ring fingers are cupped together and sweep INWARD across several strings; là sweeps back OUTWARD. In practice bō is played lighter and là follows heavier, giving a strong two-stroke flourish.
techniques.notes: Often paired with 剌 as 撥剌 bō là.

techniques.instruction_text: Là is the outward sweep of the cupped index/middle/ring fingers across several strings; it is the partner stroke to bō (inward sweep). The combined gesture 撥剌 is normally played bō-soft then là-strong as a single accented event.

Gǔn and fú form the pair 滾拂. Gǔn: the ring finger sweeps OUTWARD from a high string down toward string 1; fú: the index finger sweeps INWARD from string 1 up toward string 7. Played together they make a continuous circular wash of sound.
techniques.notes: Often paired with 拂 as 滾拂 gǔn fú.

techniques.instruction_text: Fú is the inward index-finger sweep (mǒ across many strings, low to high) that partners gǔn (outward ring-finger sweep, high to low). The combination 滾拂 runs left-inside to right-outside and back, like a circle, producing a flowing rolled texture.

Yín is a vibrato made by oscillating the stopping finger around the note position after the pluck: it begins downward then returns up, repeated 2–3 times in a narrow, gentle wave that fades. It is the lighter, smaller-amplitude vibrato.
techniques.poetic_image: Likened to a cicada singing in autumn

media.caption: Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (1/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (2/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (3/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (4/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (5/5), peiyouqin.com

techniques.instruction_text: Náo is the larger, more forceful vibrato: the stopping finger swings up then down through a WIDER arc than yín, repeated 2–3 times, strong at first and fading. Rule of thumb: yín oscillates around the note in a small wave (begins downward), náo in a broad, vigorous wave (begins upward).
techniques.poetic_image: Behold the howling ape, climbing the tall tree; wishing to go up yet not up, its momentum pressing on (瞻彼號猿，升於喬木。欲上不上，其勢逐逐)
techniques.poetic_verse: 瞻彼號猿，升於喬木。欲上不上，其勢逐逐
techniques.notes: Some teachers pronounce/relate this to 揉 róu. Abbreviated form of 猱.

media.caption: Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (1/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (2/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (3/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (4/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (5/5), peiyouqin.com

techniques.instruction_text: Chuò is an up-glide INTO the note: the finger starts roughly a hui below the target and slides up so the note arrives with a 'hidden head' (no separate attack). It pairs with zhù, the down-glide; both blend pitches smoothly, unlike 上/下 which sound two distinct pitches.

Zhù is a down-glide INTO the note: the finger starts about a hui above the target and slides down into it like flowing water, the note emerging without a separate attack. Chuò = slide up into note, zhù = slide down into note.

While the right hand plucks the string that the left hand has pressed down, the left hand glides up to the spot that is indicated. The pressing and moving of the left hand should be solid so that it will create a very clear sound. If there is one ascending after another ascending, the notation will be "二上" (Èr Shàng , up twice). In Guqin tableture, only the final destination of Èr Shàng is indicated. So the player has to listen to the tone and move his or her finger up to a proper position for the first ascending tone. Each ascending tone is approximately one whole step, for example, Do- Re- Mi, or Re-Mi-Sol, or Mi- Sol- La, or Sol-La-Do, or La-Do-Re.

opposite of "Shàng." While the right hand plucks the string that the left hand has pressed down, the left hand glides down to the spot that it is indicated. If there is one descending after another descending, the notation will be "二下" (Èr Xià, down twice). Same as Èr Shàng that the tableture only indicate the final desitination. So the player has to listen to the tone and move his or her finger down to a proper position for the first descending tone. Each descending tone is approximately one whole step. For example, Do- La- Sol, or La- Sol- Mi, or Sol- Mi-Re, or Mi- Re- Do, or Re- Do- La. The Variations of Yín and Náo:
A drawn-out vibrato movement. The frequency of up and down is several times more than Yín. The total number can be 7 to 12 times of the frequency.
A thin vibrato movement, more delicate than Yín.
A calm vibrato. It is rocking the string back and force without moving the finger.
Swinging vibrato. Similar to Shuang Zhùang (see Shuang Zhuang on next page) but slower.
Immediately vibrato. Do Yín as soon as the left hand presses the string and the right hand plays the string.
Slight / brief yin.
The movement is the same as Náo but the timing of the vibrato is longer. Same situation as Cháng Yín.
A fast Náo. Feels tight and rapid but not in a hurry.
Same situation as Lùo Zhĭ Yín. Do Náo as soon as the left hand presses the string and the right hand plays the string.

Slightly Náo. For a further study on distinguish the differences between Yín and Náo and a short film of the demo, please visit here. Main Menu / 1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / Copyright ©2001-2013 Judy (Pei-You) Chang
"Strike-nao" — a nao combined with a zhuang accent; common in early handbooks (e.g. Shen Qi Mi Pu).
Tǎng is essentially a slow 下 (xià): after the note sounds, the left hand glides downward (lower pitch) but more slowly and gently than a normal xià, giving a relaxed descending portamento.

Tuō ('tuō shàng') is essentially a slow 上 (shàng): after the note sounds, the left hand glides upward (higher pitch) more slowly than a normal shàng, a leisurely ascending portamento. It is the up-glide counterpart of tǎng.
techniques.notes: Also written 拖上 tuō shàng.

techniques.instruction_text: Zhuàng (strike-against) is done AFTER the right hand has plucked: the stopping finger snaps very quickly up (rightward) by roughly 1/5 to 1/2 of a hui-position and snaps back to the original spot, giving a sharp accent ornament. The key contrast with dòu is timing — zhuàng happens after the pluck.
"Double strike against" — perform zhuang twice.

“Xu” literally means “empty, unfilled,” therefore a “Xu Zhuàng “is to have a Zhuàng technique happen after a non- plucked sound. For example, the left hand may do a Zhuàng after an upward moving technique (Shàng or Jìng ), or a downward moving technique (Xià or Fù ) or a vibrato technique (Yín or Náo ) has been performed.

Same technique as "Zhàng" but moves the left hand very quickly down first (to the left) about 1/5 - 1/2 of to the next "Hui" position and moves back quickly to the spot indicated. It is like a faster motion of Tuì fù.

Dòu is almost identical in motion to zhuàng (a quick up-and-back of the stopping finger), but it is performed SIMULTANEOUSLY with the right-hand pluck rather than after it, so the ornament is fused into the attack of the note. Timing is the sole distinguishing feature from zhuàng.
宋成玉礀[琴書大全]: 注少許,略作猱, 而復引少許. Slides down over to the hui position a little bit, then slightly náo (once or twice), and then slides up to above the hui position a little bit.

往來得聲自上而下三次(或兩次). When a finger of the left hand presses down a string and after the right hand has pulled the string, the left hand moves down to the next sound position to the left and then moves back to where it started; and repeats this movement twice or three times to produce a total of 4 or 6 sounds. (ex. 5,3,5,3,5,3).

When a finger of the left hand presses down a string and after the right hand has pulled the string, the left hand glides up to the next Hui position to the right; and then while the right hand pulls the string again, the left hand glides back to where it started, as the action of "Zhù." Main Menu / 1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / Copyright ©2001-2013 Judy (Pei-You) Chang

Qiā qǐ is the thumb's version of dài qǐ: after the thumb stops a string and a finger presses the same string a position lower, the thumbnail plucks/lifts the string upward to sound an added note without the right hand. Pairs with 罨 in 掐撮三聲.
techniques.notes: Common in beginner pieces (e.g. 古琴吟).

techniques.instruction_text: "Claw up" — left finger plucks the string upward to sound it.

Dài qǐ: after the left ring finger has stopped and the string has sounded, the ring fingertip flicks/lifts the string as it leaves, producing an added open-string (sǎn) sound with no right-hand pluck. Contrast 掐起 (thumb) and 抓起 (thumb, lighter).

Yǎn: with NO right-hand pluck, a left-hand finger (commonly thumb, middle or ring) sharply taps/hammers the string onto the surface to produce a low, dull, dark note. It is the hammer-on counterpart to the pull-off techniques 掐起/帶起.
This technique is mostly executed with the middle or ring finger and sometimes the thumb. Same technique as Yǎn but without pressing down any string before doing Yǎn.
This technique is particularly used on the 1st string for the middle finger of the left hand. After the middle finger presses a sting down, it makes the 1st string sound by pushing it outward.
techniques.notes: Notated by the reduced glyph that reads 拙. Common in beginner pieces (e.g. 古琴吟).

techniques.instruction_text: Jìn fù (advance-and-return): after the right hand plucks a stopped note, the left hand glides UP (rightward, higher pitch) to a target position then glides back to the start, producing three sounds (one plucked, two slid) — one solid and two soft.

Tuì fù (retreat-and-return) is the mirror of jìn fù: after the pluck the left hand glides DOWN (leftward, lower pitch) to the target then returns to the start. Same three-sound, one-solid-two-soft structure, opposite direction.
"Advance" — slide up one position (component of jinfu).
"Retreat" — slide down one position.
"Lift/rise" — general term for left-hand-initiated sound; appears as a component in qiaqi, zhuaqi, daiqi.
Open-string tone (san yin). Left hand does not touch the string. Left-hand glyph component meaning "open".
Harmonic (fan yin). 泛起 fàn qǐ marks the start of a harmonics passage, 泛止 fàn zhǐ its end. Finger lightly touches the string over a hui.
techniques.notes: 泛起…泛止 bracket a harmonics section.

techniques.instruction_text: Stopped/pressed tone (an yin). Finger presses the string fully to the board.
The 13 position markers. A number (一…十三) in the glyph's upper-right gives the hui; a sub-fraction (e.g. 七六 = between hui 7 and 6, or 9.5) refines it.
String number, written as a Chinese numeral (一…七) in the glyph center, telling the right hand which string to pluck.
The combination of Muǒ and Tiaǒ, the index finger plucks the string first then strums.
The combination of Gou and Ti, the middle finger plucks the string first then strums.
The combination of Muǒ and Gou, on the same string, do Muǒ first then Gou, it creates 2 gradually continuing sounds.
Same as Muǒ Gou but faster.
On the same string, use Ti Muǒ Tiaǒ to play a fast continuing 3 sounds. Use the tip of the nails, don't put them too deep into the string.
On the same string, do Muǒ Gou first, a slowly and gradually continuing sound, then following with Ti Muǒ Tiaǒ, a faster continuing sound.

Rú Yi literally means "as one." When there are 2 strings that can be created an Àn Yin and a Săn Yin with the same sound at one time, use the middle finger or index finger (usually use the middle finger) to strum the 2 strings together.

Same as Rú Yi but play twice. Before strumming the string, position the index and middle finger above the thumb, do Ti then Tiaǒ. Some tablature will indicate a "Shuang Tán" follow by a "Rú Yi," which reminds the player that making the two previous sound as one sound. Main Menu / 1 / 2 / 3 / Copyright ©2001-2013 Judy (Pei-You) Chang
Do Bo first with lighter strength then Lá later with strong strength.
After doing Lá or Bo Lá , flatten together of the index, middle and ring finger and place them on the strings to stop the sound.
Using the thumb and middle finger (Tuo, Gou) to pluck two strings(either the 1st and 6th or the 2nd and 7th) at the same time.The strength must be the same.
The opposite of Cuo, usually used after Cuo. Using the thumb and middle finger (Bò, Ti) or the index and middle finger (Muǒ, Ti) to strum and pluck two strings together.

Use Dă, Tiăo or Gou, Tiăo back and forth on 2 strings and consisting of six notes. First Dă, then Tiăo, a slight pause, then rapidly repeat the same movement twice. Fingers must be flexible to create continuous sound.
The index finger lightly and rapidly strums out 2 or 3 strings in succession.
The combination of Guěn and Fú. Started from the left inside to the right outside and back to the left inside, like a circle. Main Menu / 1 / 2 / 3 / Copyright ©2001-2018 Judy (Pei-You) Chang
A standard three-cuō cadential figure: two strings are pinched (cuō) together, alternated with a hammer-on/pull-off ornament, then repeated to make three pinched sounds. Common at phrase endings.
A two-hand cadential cluster: the left thumb does 罨 then 掐起 while the right hand does cuō, repeated, yielding three pinched sounds. A signature ending gesture in many pieces.

The left middle or ring finger presses down a string, and the right hand plucks it, the left hand stays on the same string and does not move away yet. While the right hand plucks another string, the left hand moves either up or down to the position where it has the same sound as the string that the right hand had played. Eventually making both strings sound together (one is a solid sound, the other is a soft sound).

This technique is creating a kind of chord. The left hand plucks one string (can be the middle finger Tuīchū, or the ring finger Dàiqǐ or the thumb Zhuāqǐ), at the same time, the right hand plucks another string to make both strings sound together. Main Menu / 1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / Copyright ©2001-2013 Judy (Pei-You) Chang

Related to 應合 (yìng hé): after stopping and sounding a string, the left hand releases/slides to a new position so that a following right-hand stroke on another string meets it at a matching (usually unison or octave) pitch — the two notes joining together.
